thread.title: Task force to take on addictive social media | News | cbs19news.com
thread.title_full: Task force to take on addictive social media | News | cbs19news.com

author: News Staff
title: Task force to take on addictive social media | News | cbs19news.com
text:
RICHMOND, Va. (CBS19 NEWS) -- A new state task force will take on unrestricted cell phone use and addictive social media platforms.
Gov. Glenn Youngkin issued an executive order Empowering and Supporting Parents to Protect Their Children from Addictive Social Media and [the] Establishment of the Reclaiming Childhood Task Force on Thursday.
He calls this a critical aspect of Virginia’s youth mental health crisis.
“Virginia and the nation’s children are facing a youth mental health crisis driven by the effects of unrestricted cell phone use and addictive social media platforms. The scope of this challenge is going to require all of us, private sector, non-profit organizations and government can support parents as they work to improve their children’s mental health,” said Youngkin. “Executive Order 43 will empower parents with the tools they need to protect their children and sets the stage for a statewide response to reclaim childhood and ensure the next generation can thrive.”
This order will mobilize state agencies that provide resources for parents, educators and health care providers to help fight the negative effects of excessive social media use.
The release says this includes the development of a “Social Media and Mental Health Toolkit,” a partnership with health providers to emphasize prevention strategies and regional training for behavioral health professionals.
And the Reclaiming Childhood Task Force will bring together parents, educators, mental health experts, faith leaders, public safety officials, private sector technology experts and community leaders.
They will work to identify solutions to improve youth mental health outcomes, providing recommendations to state lawmakers and building upon the Cell Phone-Free Education in Virginia K-12 public schools efforts started by a previous executive order.
